hints.to provide: English 'provide' derives from it, but Latin 'providere' means to foresee or look ahead
hints.to see: 'To see' is 'videre' — 'providere' adds the prefix pro- meaning 'beforehand'

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'providi' — 'Provideo' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'providebo' — 'Provideo' is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'provideam' — 'Provideo' is indicative

hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see) — the base verb without the pro- prefix
hints.praebeo, praebere, praebui, praebitum: These are the parts of 'praebere' (to offer)
hints.prohibeo, prohibere, prohibui, prohibitu: These are the parts of 'prohibere' (to prevent)

steps.reveal: quanta tempestas = how great a storm (nom., subject of 'impendeat')

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'quantam tempestatem' — 'quanta tempestas' is nominative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'quanta tempestate' — this is nominative
hints.vocative: A storm is not being addressed — 'quanta tempestas' is nominative subject

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'tempestatis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'tempestatis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'tempestatis' ends in -is

hints.nominative: Nominative singular would be 'invidia' — 'invidiae' is genitive singular or dative
hints.dative: 'Invidiae' could be dative, but here it defines the type of storm — genitive of quality
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'invidia' — 'invidiae' is genitive describing 'tempestas'

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'invidiae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'invidiae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'invidiae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: What case is 'nobis' and what function does it serve?

hints.ablative of agent: Ablative of agent uses 'a/ab' — 'nobis' here is dative showing who the storm threatens
hints.dative of indirect object: Indirect object would imply giving — 'nobis' shows over whom the storm hangs
hints.ablative of accompaniment: 'Cum' + ablative expresses accompaniment — 'nobis' is dative with 'impendeat'

hints.to spend: 'To spend' is 'impendere' (same verb can mean 'spend'), but here the meaning is 'to hang over'
hints.to weigh: 'To weigh' is 'pendere' — 'impendere' with in- prefix means to hang over
hints.to impede: 'To impede' is 'impedire' — 'impendere' means to hang over or threaten

steps.reveal: impendeat = may hang over / hangs over (subjunctive in indirect question)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative would be 'impendet' — 'impendeat' with -ea- is subjunctive
hints.imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'impenderet' — 'impendeat' is present subjunctive
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'impendebit' — 'impendeat' is present subjunctive

steps.prompt: Why is the subjunctive used in the 'quanta...impendeat' clause?

hints.purpose clause: Purpose clauses use 'ut/ne' — 'quanta' introduces an indirect question requiring subjunctive
hints.result clause: Result clauses use 'ut' — 'quanta' is an interrogative adjective introducing an indirect question
hints.conditional protasis: Conditional clauses use 'si/nisi' — 'quanta' introduces an indirect question

hints.pendo, pendere, pependi, pensum: These are the parts of 'pendere' (to weigh/pay) — active transitive, different verb
hints.pendeo, pendere, pependi, —: These are the parts of 'pendere' (to hang, intransitive) — the base, but 'impendere' is the compound
hints.impedio, impedire, impedivi, impeditum: These are the parts of 'impedire' (to hinder) — different verb entirely

translation: I foresee how great a storm of hatred hangs over us
